WASHINGTON, Nov. BIT now
Heems probably that the Dawes in
dian commission will be abolished
Enough facts have developed to put
the entire commission in a very bad
light, and if the president does not
act on the recommendation of his
special commissioner, Charles j. Bona
parte. it is quite certain that con-
gressional committee would bring about
the same result. The same policy Will
result in the removal or transfer of
Indian Inspector Wright, Indian Agent
Schoenfelt, and an entire reorganiza
ton Of the management Of the terri
tory.


Mr. Bonapsrte has talked with
Chairman Bixby of the Dawes Indian
commission, and has prosecuted his in
quiries in other Why's in Washington,
and he expects to complete the inquiry
by a visit to the territory. Mean
time Secretary Hitchcock has done
some investigating on his own account
aud has discovered some things which
IL would have been better had he
known them before. Among other
things he finds that the Indian agent
